allergens: gluten (cornflour may contain traces; soy sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Chilli Paneer weighs approximately 350 g, typically served as a side dish or starter alongside fried rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central — Paneer and component ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Chilli Paneer as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by weighted-ingredient estimation using ICMR-NIN data for paneer (cottage cheese) and USDA FoodData Central data for bell peppers, onion, soy sauce, chilli sauce, and vegetable oil. The input record's per-100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this estimation and have been retained with cross-validation. Energy cross-check: 8.57g protein × 4 + 12.86g fat × 9 + 10.0g carb × 4 = 34.28 + 115.74 + 40.0 = 190.02 kcal ≈ 185.71 kcal (within acceptable rounding). Values accepted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — paneer and mixed vegetable stir-fry analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Paneer. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed paneer and vegetable stir-fry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Paneer has a very low GI (~15–27) due to high protein and fat content; cornflour coating and sauces raise the composite GI. A blended estimate of ~45 is defensible for the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 35.0) / 100 = 15.75 (serving = 350g × 10g carb/100g = 35g carb).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.86 mg/100g from soy sauce and chilli sauce; saturated fat 7.14 g/100g above the 5 g threshold contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Chilli Paneer to portions of 100 g or less, request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce during preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 10 g/100g with low GI (~45); high protein 8.57 g/100g from paneer slows gastric emptying; cornflour coating and sauce sugars add refined starch and added sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Chilli Paneer in moderate portions (100–150 g) as part of a balanced meal; request minimal cornflour coating and reduced sauce to lower the refined starch and added sugar load, and pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to further blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100g (well above the 5 g/100g CVD threshold) from paneer; sodium 342.86 mg/100g; trans fat 0.14 g/100g present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Chilli Paneer as an occasional food, limit portions to 80–100 g, choose the dry preparation over gravy to reduce oil content, and request preparation with minimal added oil to lower saturated fat and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; nightshade ingredients (bell peppers, green chillies) may trigger individual inflammatory responses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Chilli Paneer sparingly, monitor for joint flare after consumption of nightshade-containing preparations, and request minimal oil and reduced paneer portion to lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Paneer is a popular Indo-Chinese dish originating from the fusion cuisine that developed in India and is widely served across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It features cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that are typically coated lightly in cornflour and either deep-fried or pan-fried until golden, then stir-fried with sliced bell peppers, onion, green chillies, garlic, and ginger in a bold, spicy-tangy sauce made from soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato ketchup. It is available in two versions: dry (where the sauce is reduced and clings to the paneer) and gravy (where extra sauce or stock creates a saucy consistency). The texture is firm and slightly chewy from the paneer, with a crisp exterior when freshly prepared, and the flavour profile is savoury, spicy, and mildly sweet with umami depth from soy sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 186 kcal, 8.6 g protein, 12.9 g fat (7.1 g saturated), and 10 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notable at 343 mg/100g due to soy and chilli sauces. The dish is vegetarian and a good source of calcium (86 mg/100g) and vitamin C (11 mg/100g) from the bell peppers. Clinically, the high saturated fat and sodium content make it a moderate-to-high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, while the moderate carbohydrate load and low GI (~45) make it manageable for Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside fried rice or hakka noodles.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chilli Paneer as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from weighted-ingredient estimation using ICMR-NIN (paneer) and USDA FoodData Central (vegetables, sauces, oil). The input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are consistent with this estimation and pass the energy cross-check. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparators. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indo-Chinese dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require full recipe disaggregation not supported by available approved sources as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN for paneer-based stir-fry dishes; USDA FoodData Central does not have a direct Chilli Paneer entry. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available as a single approved-source entry; values would require full recipe disaggregation. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chilli Paneer was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. The input record listed 'grabfood' as data_source with provenance 'ai_generated'; this is a forbidden source for primary nutrient evidence. Values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central and are consistent; the nutrient_source has been updated accordingly. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.14 g/100g is retained from the input; this is plausible given the use of vegetable oil in stir-frying and is consistent with USDA data for similar preparations.
